retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Issues of Concern -- Mount-Hume Classification System
retrieved_docs.content: More than 100 years later, Mount and Hume published a caries classification system following the current and more conservative approach to caries. [3] The Mount-Hume classification system incorporated the advent of fluoride and adhesive restorative materials, the use of which modified the old principles of cavity design. [3] Mount and Hume classified caries based on their site and size. [2] (see Table 2. Mount-Hume Site Classification of Caries. and Table 3. Mount-Hume Size Classification of Caries.) The size classification was updated in 2006 to include Size 0, referring to non-cavitated lesions, and modify the definition of Size 1. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Various classification methods for dental carious lesions have been developed, each with advantages and limitations. Newer classification systems, like the International Caries Detection and Assessment System (ICDAS) and the American Dental Association Caries Classification System (ADA CCS), have evolved from the traditional Black classification to include the current understanding of the carious process, maximize healthy tooth surfaces, and prevent extension of the carious lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Pathophysiology -- Microbial Role
retrieved_docs.content: Carious lesions have a higher percentage and incidence of certain bacterial species: Streptococcus mutans , Streptococcus sobrinus , and Lactobacilli , isolated from advanced caries. [9] The specific plaque hypothesis was based on this finding, believing only specific bacteria caused the disease. [10] Indeed, Streptococcus mutans, in particular, are strongly linked to caries, but caries can develop in sites where Streptococcus mutans are absent. [3] This observation gave rise to the nonspecific plaque hypothesis, where caries is believed to result from the net metabolic activity of the microbiota of the biofilm. Although the etiology of dental caries is not completely specific, we cannot ignore that a limited type of bacteria is consistently found in higher numbers from the affected areas. [11] The currently accepted concept is the ecological plaque hypothesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Classification Systems -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries is a prevalent chronic disease. If left untreated, caries may progress to tooth destruction and exodontia. Dental caries is a complex multifactorial disease of individual, biological, behavioral, and environmental factors. [1] The clinical sign of dental carious is a lesion of varying severity, ranging from opacity in the enamel to frank cavitation exposing dentin. Accurately diagnosing dental caries promotes effective treatment planning, prevents disease progression, and supports optimal patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 700 species of microorganisms colonize the human oral cavity. [1] These bacteria inhabiting the human oral cavity are mainly commensals along with a sparse population of pathogenic bacteria. [2] Periodontitis is one of the most common ailments affecting the teeth, leading to the destruction of the supporting and surrounding tooth structure. [3] The term "periodontitis" is build up of two words, i.e., "periodont-" meaning "structure surrounding the teeth" and "itis" means "inflammation." Periodontitis is originally a disease originating from the gingival tissue which if left untreated results in penetration of inflammation to the deeper tissues, altering the bone homeostasis causing tooth loss. [3] Periodontal disease has a multifactorial origin. [4] The main culprit identified in periodontitis is the bacterial biofilm growing on the tooth surfaces. [5] [6] While the host response determines the progression of the disease along with factors like local factors like plaque and calculus, genetics, environmental factors, systemic health of the patient, lifestyle habits and various social determinants also play a role. [4] The deleterious effects of periodontopathogens are not limited to the periodontium, but they also exude their ill effects on the systemic health of the patients. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Function -- Pathophysiology of Dental Caries
retrieved_docs.content: All the tooth surfaces are susceptible to caries throughout an individual's lifetime. But, the disease will not occur unless teeth are exposed to cariogenic biofilm and frequent sugar consumption. The modern concept of caries also considers other factors, such as sociodemographic situation, access to dental care, and fluoride exposure, which are significant but not decisive to develop the disease. For all of this, dental caries should be described as a complex biofilm-mediated affection linked mostly to frequent ingestion of sugars and poor oral hygiene combined with inadequate fluoride exposure. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Poor dental and oral health can potentially lead to numerous issues. Of particular concern is plaque, a biofilm of various bacteria (mainly Streptococcus and anaerobes) that forms on the surface of teeth. Brushing alone does not always remove plaque in its entirety. If plaque is not professionally removed regularly, periodontal problems and gingivitis can set in. Plaque can mineralize, eventually leading to tartar build-up, causing gingivitis, halitosis, and dental caries.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Pathophysiology -- Microbial Role
retrieved_docs.content: The ecological plaque hypothesis believes that dental caries is not caused by a specific type of microorganism acting alone but is the result of a shift in the microbiota of the dental biofilm towards more cariogenic species. [3] Oral acidic conditions from regular sugar consumption select the bacteria that empathize more with this environment and eliminate the benign species that do not tolerate such conditions. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The dental role of hydroxyapatite is significant, as it constitutes 70% to 80% of the weight of dentin and enamel. Enamel, the hardest substance in the human body, is made up of relatively large hydroxyapatite crystals measuring 25 nm thick, 40 to 120 nm wide, and 160 to 1000 nm long. [6] Unlike bone, enamel lacks collagen; instead, amelogenins and enamelins provide a framework for mineralization. Hydroxyapatite is the primary component of enamel, helping to minimize diffuse reflectivity by filling surface pores, which contributes to the semitranslucent appearance of enamel. [4] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As a healthcare provider, it is important to discuss with patients the concept of proper oral hygiene and its importance in preventing dental caries, periodontal disease, halitosis, and gingivitis. Despite careful brushing and flossing, plaque buildup and tartar can form and must be periodically cleaned professionally. It is important to advise patients to brush twice a day to prevent the formation of plaque and tartar. Flossing should be done once per day, as it removes plaque from between the teeth and prevents periodontal disease. [36] Oral health should be incorporated into routine primary care health through counseling about diet, oral hygiene, smoking cessation, and screening for dental disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dentistry dates back to 5000 B.C. when the thinking was that the cause of dental caries was a “tooth worm.” The term “dental caries” was first reported in the literature around 1634, and it originates from the Latin word “caries,” which stands for decay. The term was initially used to describe holes in the teeth. Dental caries is reported to be one of the oldest and most common diseases found in humans. Dental caries is a prevalent chronic infectious disease resulting from tooth-adherent cariogenic bacteria that metabolize sugars to produce acid, which, over time, demineralizes tooth structure.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The initial layer deposited on the surface of teeth in the formation of dental plaque is "acquired pellicle." [20] This layer is formed within seconds of the exposure of tooth surfaces, followed by the initial attachment of the early colonizers of the biofilm. Streptococcus and Actinomyces species are the primary colonizers which are gram-positive, facultative bacteria. [20] The "adhesin receptors" present on the surface of primary colonizers bind with the proline-rich proteins of the pellicle. This binding leads to revealing of receptor sites known as "cryptitope" which further leads to coaggregation. [21] Gradually there is a layer by layer deposition of dental plaque leading to a deficiency of oxygen in the environment eventually leading to colonization of anaerobic bacteria. [22] The bridging microbe between primary and secondary colonizers is Fusobacterium species. [22] The gradual shift of these aerobic conditions to anaerobic condition marks the progression of gingivitis to periodontitis. Socransky et al. in a study divided the microbes into microbial complexes based on colors. [23] [5] Red and orange complexes bacteria present in the subgingival area are intricately associated with periodontal disease. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Management of Patients Undergoing Radiation Therapy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Pre-radiation dental treatment is done to prevent further infection and avoid the need for invasive procedures during and after radiotherapy. All efforts are directed at minimizing the risk and severity of head and neck radiotherapy complications: oral mucositis, oropharyngeal candidiasis, xerostomia, radiation-related caries, and osteoradionecrosis. During this stage, patients must be informed of the importance of maintaining excellent oral hygiene and given careful oral care instructions and dietary advice. All carious lesions must be restored. Scaling, oral prophylaxis, and periodontal therapy should be performed. All sharp cusps and restorations should be smoothed. [2] If the patient wears dentures, these should be checked to ensure they are well-fitted and not at risk of causing ulceration. If a definitive restoration cannot be placed due to time limitations, carious tissue must be removed and teeth restored with glass ionomer cement. Amalgam restorations should be avoided as they often backscatter and cause local mucosal irritation. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The initial treatment of periodontitis is based on non-surgical measures. Scaling and root planing significantly reduce the subgingival microbial burden by removing dental biofilm, calculus, and bacterial endotoxins. [5] Oral hygiene instruction plays a central role in the non-surgical treatment of periodontitis and aims to control biofilm through frequent mechanical removal by the patient. [6] Periodontal local and systemic risk factors, such as overhanging restorations, occlusal interferences, and tobacco smoking, must be addressed in this phase of treatment. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Reconstructing damaged hard tissue is essential for various reasons, including traumatic or nontraumatic events, congenital abnormalities, and disease. Such damage can significantly impact orthopedic, dental, and maxillofacial surgeries. Research on numerous biomaterials shows that calcium phosphates have been utilized in hard tissue reconstruction for over 6 decades, with hydroxyapatite being the primary material used in orthopedics and dentistry.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries is a term that refers to both the disease and the resulting lesion. The caries process occurs in the biofilm, which is permanently active with every pH fluctuation [1] , and the lesion manifests in the dental hard tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: adverse‑event reporting and clinical management. Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, the designation “Allowed qualifier” is applied within the scope of enzymology to the conditions قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), intestinal infection, and اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder). This indicates that enzyme‑related criteria or measurements are permissible as qualifiers when evaluating these gastrointestinal and oral disorders, thereby supporting more precise diagnostic assessment and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: electrolyte solutions, SMQ‑defined disorders), infectious disease (antibiotic granules), nutrition (supplements), and oral health (fluoride dentifrices). Their dual roles as active agents and excipients underscore their broad importance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology is identified as an applicable qualifier for the oral conditions قيلة مثانية, قيلة مستقيمية, and اضطراب اللسان, indicating that microbiological factors are relevant in the assessment or management of these disorders. Bacterial vaccines are, in turn, an applicable qualifier for microbiology, reflecting the role of immunization strategies within the broader microbiological context. The term “Without” is noted as not being associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that the absence of a particular factor does not have a direct relationship with these specific conditions. Together, these connections highlight the importance of microbiological considerations and vaccine‑related interventions in certain oral pathologies, while clarifying that “Without” does not contribute to their etiology or presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2816
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic approach labeled “Allowed qualifier” is indicated for a range of conditions, including acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة), various injury‑ and infection‑related cases (حالات الإصابات والعدوى), chronic fungal infection (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ), and pathological adhesion (الالتصاق). This therapy is therefore applicable across both immunologic, infectious, and tissue‑related disorders, highlighting its broad relevance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2771
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is relevant in the epidemiological context of several disorders, including قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), intestinal infection, اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), and تمزق الشبكية (retinal detachment). Recognizing that the allowed qualifier pertains to the epidemiology of these conditions highlights the importance of monitoring their occurrence, distribution, and risk factors within populations, thereby supporting public‑health planning, disease surveillance, and targeted preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 206
retrieved_docs.content: open wounds without complication and to open wounds of the extremities, indicating that several open wounds may evolve into complications, especially when extremities are involved. Overall, the framework integrates firearm‑related injuries, accidental dental loss, and a spectrum of cutting‑instrument injuries under unified e‑code classifications (“firearm” and “cut/pierceb”). It emphasizes that these mechanisms commonly produce open traumatic wounds—single (e.g., nail wound) or multiple—and that such wounds can progress to complications, guiding consistent injury documentation, epidemiologic tracking, and targeted clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 5 -- Community 2956
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an “Allowed qualifier” is linked through the relationship “enzymologie” to three specific clinical conditions: اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), فالج (paralysis), and قرحات الطرفين السفليين (ulcers of the lower limbs). This indicates that enzymological considerations are recognized as permissible qualifiers when evaluating these disorders, suggesting that enzyme‑related mechanisms or assessments may be relevant in the diagnosis, pathophysiology, or management of tongue disturbances, paralysis, and lower‑extremity ulcerations.
